headache_fever.severity: Often due to viral infections like common cold or flu, but can rarely indicate something serious.
immediateSteps: Rest in a cool, quiet, and dark room to reduce discomfort.
Drink plenty of water, ORS, or clear fluids to prevent dehydration.
Use a cold, damp cloth on the forehead and neck to reduce temperature.
Avoid strenuous physical activity until the fever and headache settle.
If you already use paracetamol safely, you may take a standard adult dose as advised previously by your doctor.
whenToSeekHelp: Fever lasts more than 3 days or keeps coming back.
Severe, sudden headache with neck stiffness, confusion, or vomiting.
Rash, breathing difficulty, chest pain, or seizures.
You have other serious conditions (like uncontrolled diabetes, heart disease, or are pregnant).
headache_fever.specialist: General physician or internal medicine doctor
headache_fever.disclaimer: This is general information only and not a diagnosis. Always consult a doctor or visit the nearest hospital, especially if symptoms are severe or worsening.

chest_pain.severity: Chest pain can be serious, especially if sudden, severe, or associated with breathlessness.
immediateSteps: Stop all activity and sit or lie in a comfortable position with head elevated.
Stay calm and avoid walking or climbing stairs.
If you have been prescribed heart tablets (like sorbitrate) in the past, use them only as advised by your cardiologist.
Do not drive yourself to the hospital; ask someone to help or call an ambulance.
whenToSeekHelp: Pain is crushing, squeezing, or spreads to arm, jaw, back, or neck.
There is shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, or feeling like you might faint.
Pain lasts more than 10–15 minutes or keeps coming back.
You have a history of heart disease, high BP, diabetes, or high cholesterol.
chest_pain.disclaimer: Treat any worrying chest pain as an emergency. Call local emergency services (such as 112 in India) or go to the nearest hospital immediately.

stomach_pain.severity: Many causes are mild (gas, acidity), but some can be serious like appendicitis or gallbladder problems.
immediateSteps: Avoid heavy, oily, or spicy food until the pain settles.
Drink small sips of water frequently; avoid carbonated drinks and alcohol.
Use a warm water bag on the abdomen for gentle relief, if it feels comfortable.
Note what makes the pain better or worse (food, movement, lying down).
whenToSeekHelp: Severe pain, pain in the lower right side, or pain that keeps increasing.
Persistent vomiting, blood in vomit or stool, or black/tarry stool.
High fever, yellow eyes, difficulty passing urine, or bloating with no gas passing.
Pain after an injury, fall, or during pregnancy.
stomach_pain.specialist: General physician, gastroenterologist, or emergency doctor
stomach_pain.disclaimer: Do not ignore strong or persistent abdominal pain. Get examined by a doctor to rule out serious conditions.

breathing_difficulty.problem: Breathing Difficulty or Shortness of Breath
breathing_difficulty.severity: Breathing trouble can be dangerous and must be taken seriously.
immediateSteps: Sit upright; do not lie flat. Support your back with pillows or a wall.
Loosen tight clothing around neck and chest area.
Use your prescribed inhaler or nebuliser if you are an asthma/COPD patient, as previously advised by your doctor.
Try to stay calm and breathe slowly in through the nose and out through the mouth.
whenToSeekHelp: Breathlessness comes suddenly, is severe, or associated with chest pain.
Lips or face look blue, or you cannot speak full sentences.
You hear a loud wheeze, or inhalers are not giving relief.
There is high fever, confusion, or you feel you may faint.
breathing_difficulty.disclaimer: Any significant breathing problem is an emergency. Seek urgent medical help or call local emergency services immediately.

minor_injury.severity: Most small cuts and bruises are minor and can be managed at home with basic first-aid.
immediateSteps: Wash your hands first, then gently clean the area with clean water.
For cuts, apply gentle pressure with a clean cloth or gauze to stop bleeding.
Rinse with mild soap around (not deep inside) the wound and pat dry.
Apply an antiseptic solution or cream if available and keep the area clean and dry.
For bruises, you may apply a cold pack wrapped in cloth for 10–15 minutes.
whenToSeekHelp: Wound is deep, very dirty, or edges are far apart.
Bleeding does not stop after 10–15 minutes of pressure.
There is loss of movement or sensation around the injured area.
You have not had a tetanus shot in the last 5–10 years or are unsure.
minor_injury.specialist: General physician or emergency doctor for larger wounds
minor_injury.disclaimer: Basic first-aid is helpful, but see a doctor if you are unsure about the depth, cleanliness, or need for stitches or tetanus protection.

diarrhea_dehydration_child.problem: Child with Diarrhea and Risk of Dehydration
diarrhea_dehydration_child.severity: Common in rural areas due to infections or unsafe water and can become dangerous if dehydration develops.
immediateSteps: Give ORS (oral rehydration solution) in small sips frequently; continue breastfeeding if the child is an infant.
Avoid giving only plain water, fizzy drinks, or very sweet juices as the main fluid.
Offer light, soft foods like khichdi, dal-rice, or banana if the child is willing to eat.
Keep the child in a cool, shaded place and watch urine output and activity level.
whenToSeekHelp: Child passes very frequent watery stools or vomits everything.
There is sunken eyes, very dry mouth, no tears while crying, or no urine for 6 hours or more.
Child is very sleepy, difficult to wake, or extremely irritable.
There is blood in stool, high fever, or the child is younger than 6 months.
diarrhea_dehydration_child.specialist: Pediatrician or nearest child health centre
diarrhea_dehydration_child.disclaimer: Severe dehydration in children is an emergency. Reach the nearest hospital or health centre as soon as possible.

high_fever_chills.severity: In rural and tropical regions this can be due to malaria, dengue, or other serious infections.
immediateSteps: Check temperature if a thermometer is available and note the readings.
Use light clothing and sponge the body with lukewarm water to bring down fever.
Encourage regular intake of water, ORS, or clear soups to prevent dehydration.
Avoid self-starting antibiotics without doctor advice.
whenToSeekHelp: Fever persists beyond 2–3 days or is very high (above 102°F / 38.9°C).
There is severe headache, vomiting, abdominal pain, rash, or bleeding from gums or nose.
The person becomes drowsy, confused, or has seizures.
There is a known outbreak of malaria or dengue in the village.
high_fever_chills.specialist: General physician or infectious disease specialist
high_fever_chills.disclaimer: Fever with chills can indicate serious infections. Early testing and treatment greatly improve outcomes.

snake_bite.severity: Snake bites, especially from venomous species, are life-threatening rural emergencies.
immediateSteps: Keep the person calm and lying down; keep the bitten limb below heart level and as still as possible.
Remove tight items like rings, bangles, or shoes near the bite due to swelling.
Do not cut, suck, burn, or apply chemicals to the wound and do not give alcohol.
If possible, remember the snake’s colour and pattern but do not try to catch or kill it.
whenToSeekHelp: Any suspected snake bite should be treated as an emergency.
There is swelling around the bite, difficulty breathing, drooping eyelids, or weakness.
There is bleeding from gums, vomiting of blood, or passing black stool.
The person becomes drowsy, confused, or collapses.
snake_bite.specialist: Emergency department with anti-snake venom facilities
snake_bite.disclaimer: Transport the patient urgently to the nearest hospital with anti-snake venom. Do not rely only on home remedies or traditional methods.

dog_bite_rabies_risk.severity: Rabies is almost always fatal but completely preventable with proper vaccination after exposure.
immediateSteps: Immediately wash the wound thoroughly with running water and soap for at least 10–15 minutes.
Do not tightly bandage; allow it to bleed slightly and keep it clean.
Avoid applying chilli, lime, soil, or other irritants on the wound.
Note the animal’s behaviour and if possible, whether it is vaccinated or can be observed.
whenToSeekHelp: Any bite or scratch that breaks the skin from a dog, cat, monkey, or wild animal.
Bites on the face, neck, or multiple deep wounds.
The animal is unknown, stray, unvaccinated, or acting strangely.
You are unsure about your last rabies vaccination or tetanus shot.
dog_bite_rabies_risk.specialist: Rabies vaccination centre or hospital emergency department
dog_bite_rabies_risk.disclaimer: Post-exposure rabies vaccination and sometimes immunoglobulin are urgent. Visit a hospital or anti-rabies centre as soon as possible.

scorpion_insect_sting.severity: Many stings are mild but some scorpion stings or multiple stings can be dangerous.
immediateSteps: Move the person to a safe area away from the insect or scorpion.
Wash the site with soap and water and remove any visible stinger by scraping gently.
Apply a cold pack wrapped in cloth for 10–15 minutes to reduce pain and swelling.
Keep the affected limb elevated and avoid tight bands around it.
whenToSeekHelp: There is severe pain, spreading redness, or swelling involving a large area.
The person develops difficulty breathing, wheezing, swelling of face or lips, or feels faint.
Sting is on the face, tongue, or inside the mouth.
The person is a child, elderly, or has heart/lung disease.
scorpion_insect_sting.disclaimer: Allergic reactions and some scorpion stings can be life-threatening. Do not delay seeking medical care if serious symptoms appear.

heat_stroke_exhaustion.severity: Common in rural outdoor workers during summer and can quickly become life-threatening.
immediateSteps: Move the person to a cool, shaded or indoor area immediately.
Remove excess clothing and cool the body with wet cloths, cool water spray, or a fan.
Give cool water or ORS to sip slowly if the person is fully conscious.
Lay the person on their side if drowsy to prevent choking on vomit.
whenToSeekHelp: Body feels extremely hot, the person is confused, drowsy, or unconscious.
There is no sweating despite the heat or there are seizures.
Vomiting repeatedly or unable to drink fluids.
Symptoms started after long work in fields, construction, or travel in heat.
heat_stroke_exhaustion.disclaimer: Heat stroke is a medical emergency: rapid cooling and hospital treatment are essential to prevent organ damage.

hypothermia_cold_exposure.problem: Exposure to Cold and Possible Hypothermia
hypothermia_cold_exposure.severity: Prolonged exposure to cold or rain can lead to dangerously low body temperature.
immediateSteps: Move the person to a warm, dry shelter and remove wet clothing.
Wrap in dry blankets or warm clothes, including head, hands, and feet.
Offer warm (not very hot) sweet drinks if the person is awake and able to swallow.
Warm the body gradually; avoid direct high heat like very hot water or fire close to the skin.
whenToSeekHelp: Person is very drowsy, confused, or not responding normally.
Shivering stops suddenly and the person feels very cold to touch.
There are breathing difficulties, slow pulse, or stiff muscles.
Person is very young, very old, or has long exposure in rain, flood, or cold.
hypothermia_cold_exposure.disclaimer: Severe hypothermia is life-threatening. Seek hospital care urgently while continuing gentle rewarming.

pregnancy_bleeding.severity: Any bleeding in pregnancy can be serious for the mother or baby.
immediateSteps: Make the pregnant woman lie on her side and keep her as calm and still as possible.
Do not insert anything into the vagina and do not attempt to check internally.
Note the amount of bleeding (pads soaked, clots) and any associated pain or dizziness.
Ensure someone reliable is ready to accompany her to a hospital.
whenToSeekHelp: Any visible bleeding in pregnancy, even if small.
Severe abdominal pain, cramps, passing clots, or fluid leakage.
Feeling faint, breathless, or severe weakness.
No fetal movements felt after 7 months of pregnancy when previously active.
pregnancy_bleeding.disclaimer: Bleeding during pregnancy must be evaluated urgently by a specialist to protect both mother and baby.

labour_emergency.problem: Onset of Labour or Delivery at Home
labour_emergency.severity: Most labours are natural but complications can arise, especially without skilled help.
immediateSteps: Keep the woman in a clean, private place with support persons she trusts.
Encourage deep breathing and comfortable positions; do not panic.
If waters break, note the time and colour of fluid (clear, green, or blood-stained).
Arrange immediate transport to the nearest facility with delivery services.
whenToSeekHelp: Strong contractions less than 5 minutes apart for more than an hour.
Heavy bleeding, severe pain, or the baby’s parts (hand/foot/cord) coming out first.
Foul-smelling discharge, high fever, or seizure in pregnancy.
No progress in labour for many hours or previous history of difficult delivery.
labour_emergency.specialist: Obstetrician or skilled birth attendant at a maternity hospital
labour_emergency.disclaimer: Institutional delivery is safest. Seek skilled help early to prevent complications for mother and baby.

pesticide_poisoning.severity: Common rural emergency with very high risk of death if not treated quickly.
immediateSteps: Move the person away from the source and into fresh air; avoid breathing fumes yourself.
Do not make the person vomit unless specifically instructed by a doctor.
Remove contaminated clothing and wash exposed skin with plenty of water.
If safe, carry the pesticide container or photo of its label to the hospital.
whenToSeekHelp: Any suspected swallowing, inhalation, or skin exposure to pesticides or unknown chemicals.
There is vomiting, stomach pain, excessive salivation, sweating, or difficulty breathing.
The person becomes drowsy, confused, has fits, or is unconscious.
Exposure was intentional (self-harm) regardless of amount.
pesticide_poisoning.specialist: Emergency department with poison management facilities
pesticide_poisoning.disclaimer: Pesticide poisoning is a critical emergency. Take the patient to the nearest hospital immediately; do not delay for home remedies.

seizure_fits.severity: Seizures can result from epilepsy, fever, infections, or head injury and may be life-threatening.
immediateSteps: Lay the person on their side on the floor, away from sharp or hard objects.
Do not put anything in the mouth and do not try to hold the tongue.
Loosen tight clothing around the neck and keep the airway clear.
Time how long the seizure lasts and observe the body movements.
whenToSeekHelp: Seizure lasts more than 3–5 minutes or repeats without full recovery in between.
This is the first-ever seizure or it follows a head injury or poisoning.
Person does not wake up or breathe normally after the jerking stops.
Seizure occurs in a pregnant woman, infant, or person with known serious illness.
seizure_fits.disclaimer: Prompt medical evaluation after a seizure is important to find and treat the underlying cause.

head_injury_fall.problem: Head Injury After Fall or Accident
head_injury_fall.severity: Head injuries can cause internal bleeding even if the outside wound looks small.
immediateSteps: If the person is unconscious, check breathing and place them on their side if breathing is normal.
Do not move the neck unnecessarily; support the head and neck in a straight line.
Control any external bleeding with gentle pressure using a clean cloth.
Do not give food, drink, or medicines until fully alert and checked by a doctor.
whenToSeekHelp: Loss of consciousness, confusion, repeated vomiting, or seizures.
Bleeding from ears or nose or clear watery fluid leaking.
Severe headache, unequal pupils, or weakness of arms/legs.
Fall from height, road traffic accident, or injury with heavy object.
head_injury_fall.disclaimer: Serious head injuries may not show immediately. Always get evaluated after a significant fall or blow to the head.

fracture_suspected.severity: Fractures need proper alignment and immobilisation to heal correctly.
immediateSteps: Support and immobilise the injured limb using a splint (wooden stick, cardboard) padded with cloth.
Do not try to straighten a deformed limb forcefully.
Apply a cold pack wrapped in cloth to reduce pain and swelling.
Keep the person comfortable and avoid walking on an injured leg.
whenToSeekHelp: Visible deformity, bone protruding out, or inability to move or bear weight.
Severe pain, swelling, or abnormal movement at the injury site.
Numbness, tingling, or pale, cold fingers or toes beyond the injury.
Fracture suspected in children, elderly, or after major trauma.
fracture_suspected.disclaimer: Fractures should be X-rayed and treated by a doctor to prevent long-term disability.

burn_injury.problem: Burn from Heat, Flame, or Electricity
burn_injury.severity: Burns can lead to infection, fluid loss, and scarring, especially in children.
immediateSteps: Immediately cool the burn under cool running water for at least 15–20 minutes.
Remove rings, bangles, or tight clothing near the burnt area before swelling starts.
Do not apply toothpaste, ink, mud, or butter on the burn.
Cover the area loosely with a clean, non-fluffy cloth or sterile dressing.
whenToSeekHelp: Burns on face, hands, feet, genitals, or major joints.
Large burns, deep burns, or burns caused by electricity or chemicals.
Burns in children, elderly, or pregnant women.
Signs of infection later like pus, foul smell, or fever.
burn_injury.disclaimer: Serious burns require early hospital treatment to reduce complications and scarring.

eye_foreign_body.problem: Foreign Body or Injury in the Eye
eye_foreign_body.severity: Eye injuries can threaten sight if not handled correctly.
immediateSteps: Do not rub the eye; ask the person to blink several times.
Rinse the eye gently with clean water or saline if available.
If an object is stuck on the surface, do not try to remove it with fingers or tools.
Cover the injured eye lightly with a clean cloth and keep both eyes as still as possible.
whenToSeekHelp: Persistent pain, redness, or sensation of something in the eye.
Sudden loss of vision, double vision, or bleeding from the eye.
Injury from metal, glass, chemical, or high-speed particles (like grinding/welding).
Any injury in a child or if vision seems reduced.
eye_foreign_body.disclaimer: Delicate eye structures heal poorly from rough handling. Always let a specialist examine significant eye injuries.

ear_infection_pain.problem: Ear Pain or Suspected Ear Infection
ear_infection_pain.severity: Ear infections are common in children and can affect hearing if untreated.
immediateSteps: Keep the ear dry; do not pour oil, water, or herbal liquids inside.
Give age-appropriate pain relief only if previously advised by a doctor.
Make the child rest with the painful ear slightly elevated.
Note any fever, discharge, or recent cold/cough.
whenToSeekHelp: Severe ear pain, high fever, or swelling behind the ear.
Pus or blood coming out from the ear canal.
Child seems not to hear sounds properly or is very irritable.
Ear pain after injury or insertion of foreign objects.
ear_infection_pain.disclaimer: Avoid inserting objects or home remedies into the ear. Medical assessment helps prevent long-term hearing problems.

tooth_dental_pain.severity: Dental infections can spread to jaw or face if ignored.
immediateSteps: Rinse the mouth gently with warm salt water.
Avoid very hot, cold, or sweet foods on the painful side.
Use pain relief only as previously recommended by a doctor or dentist.
Keep the head elevated when lying down to reduce throbbing.
whenToSeekHelp: Persistent toothache for more than 1–2 days.
Swelling of face, jaw, or difficulty opening the mouth.
Fever, foul taste, or pus discharge in the mouth.
Difficulty swallowing or breathing.
tooth_dental_pain.disclaimer: Painkillers give temporary relief only. Dental problems need proper treatment like filling or extraction.

urine_burning_uti.severity: Urinary infections are common, especially in women, and can spread to the kidneys if untreated.
immediateSteps: Drink extra clean water throughout the day unless your doctor has restricted fluids.
Do not hold urine for long periods; pass urine whenever you feel the urge.
Maintain good personal hygiene and wipe from front to back after using the toilet.
Avoid very spicy foods and very sugary drinks temporarily.
whenToSeekHelp: Burning or pain persists for more than 1–2 days.
There is fever, back pain, nausea, or vomiting.
You notice blood in urine or foul smell.
You are pregnant, diabetic, or have kidney disease.
urine_burning_uti.disclaimer: Urinary infections usually need antibiotics chosen by a doctor. Do not self-medicate repeatedly without proper tests.

anemia_weakness.severity: Very common in rural populations due to poor diet, blood loss, or chronic illness.
immediateSteps: Rest adequately and avoid working continuously in heat or heavy labour.
Take iron-rich foods if available such as green leafy vegetables, jaggery, pulses, eggs, and meat.
Drink plenty of clean water and avoid skipping meals.
If you already have iron or vitamin tablets prescribed, continue them regularly.
whenToSeekHelp: Extreme tiredness, breathlessness on small activity, or chest discomfort.
Very pale tongue, palms, or inside of eyelids.
History of heavy menstrual bleeding, blood in stool, or chronic illness like kidney or heart disease.
Dizziness, frequent fainting, or very fast heartbeat.
anemia_weakness.disclaimer: Blood tests are needed to confirm anaemia and its cause. Long-term weakness should not be ignored.

child_fever_simple.problem: Fever in a Child (Likely Simple Viral)
child_fever_simple.severity: Most childhood fevers are due to viral infections and settle in a few days but must be watched closely.
immediateSteps: Dress the child in light clothing and keep the room comfortably cool.
Give plenty of fluids like water, ORS, breast milk, or light soups.
Use fever medicine (like paracetamol) only in correct dose as per previous doctor advice.
Monitor temperature and observe for rashes, breathing difficulty, or unusual behaviour.
whenToSeekHelp: Fever in a baby younger than 3 months.
Fever more than 101°F (38.3°C) lasting longer than 2–3 days.
Child is very irritable, difficult to wake, has a stiff neck, or seizures.
There is persistent vomiting, rash, or poor feeding.
child_fever_simple.disclaimer: Though many fevers are mild, infants and small children can deteriorate quickly. When in doubt, consult a doctor.
